title: Union types — either/or values
instruction:
A union type `A | B` means a value can be type A or type B. You must narrow it before using type-specific methods.

Process a value that might be a string or number.
successMessage: Inside `if (typeof value === "string")`, TypeScript narrows to `string`. In the else branch it's `number`. No unsafe casting.

title: Literal union types — closed sets of values
instruction:
A union of literal types restricts values to a specific set. TypeScript ensures only valid values are used.

Create a typed status system.
successMessage: `Record<Status, string>` requires an entry for every status. Add a new status to the union and TypeScript immediately tells you to add its icon.

title: Discriminated unions — type-safe variants
instruction:
A discriminated union uses a shared `kind` or `type` field to distinguish variants. TypeScript narrows automatically in switch statements.

Handle different notification shapes.
successMessage: In `case "modal"`, `.actions` is accessible. In `case "banner"`, `.level` is accessible. TypeScript narrows to the exact variant in each branch.

title: Union with null — nullable values
instruction:
`T | null` and `T | undefined` are the standard way to express optional values. TypeScript forces you to handle the missing case before using the value.

Safely handle nullable values.
successMessage: After `if (user === null) return`, TypeScript knows `user` is non-null for the rest of the function. No optional chaining needed — the early return guarantees the value exists.

title: Union narrowing with type predicates
instruction:
A function returning `value is Type` is a type guard — it narrows the type at the call site when it returns `true`.

Write type guards for API responses.
successMessage: After `if (isSuccess(r))`, TypeScript narrows `r` to `Success<T>` — `.value` is accessible. In the else branch it's `Failure` — `.error` and `.code` are accessible.

title: Intersection types — combine constraints
instruction:
An intersection type `A & B` requires a value to satisfy BOTH types simultaneously. Use it to compose reusable type pieces.

Build a type-safe entity system with intersection types.
successMessage: `formatAudit` accepts any object with `id` and timestamps — not just `User`. Adding new entity types (Order, Product) is automatic if they include the required mixins.

title: Template literal union types
instruction:
Template literal types generate all combinations of string literals. They're used for event names, CSS properties, and API routes.

Create typed event names.
successMessage: `EventName` is all 9 combinations. `"payment.failed"` doesn't exist in the type — TypeScript catches typos and invalid events at compile time.

title: Mapped type from union — transform all variants
instruction:
You can map over a union type to transform it, creating related types automatically.

Generate action types from a union.
successMessage: `CssVar<Color>` generates `"--red" | "--green" | "--blue" | "--yellow"`. The `Record<ColorVar, string>` requires all four CSS variables. Add a new color to the union and TypeScript immediately tells you to add its value.

title: Union narrowing with switch exhaustion
instruction:
Using `never` in the default case ensures all union variants are handled. Add a new variant and TypeScript errors if you forget to handle it.

Build an exhaustive reducer.
successMessage: The `default: never` pattern is exhaustive checking. If you add `| { type: "multiply"; factor: number }` to `AppAction` and forget its case, TypeScript errors — `action` would no longer be `never` in the default.

title: Tagged union vs enum — when each fits
instruction:
Tagged unions and enums solve similar problems. Tagged unions are more flexible and idiomatic TypeScript; enums provide namespace and reverse mapping.

Compare both approaches for a state machine.
successMessage: Enums are great for state machines because the namespace (`TrafficLight.Red`) is clear and the switch exhaustively checks all states. Tagged unions are better when each state carries different data.

title: Conditional types with unions — distributive behavior
instruction:
Conditional types distribute over unions — applying to each member separately. This enables powerful type filtering.

Filter and transform union types.
successMessage: Conditional types distribute over unions — `ExcludeNull<A | B | C>` = `ExcludeNull<A> | ExcludeNull<B> | ExcludeNull<C>`. This is the exact implementation of TypeScript's built-in `NonNullable<T>`.

title: Union types in practice — real patterns
instruction:
Union types are everywhere in real TypeScript codebases. Here's a practical example combining several patterns.

Model a complete user authentication state.
successMessage: Each state carries only the data that makes sense for it — `authenticated` has `user` and `token`, `error` has `message` and `retryable`. This is the standard pattern for UI state management in TypeScript.
